message:
chore(beads): file 2 reality-check beads from README/code audit (cc_1)

ft-xaych: README's Mission & Tx Control section says "Tx lifecycle
commands currently ship under robot mode" but the CLI exposes
`ft tx plan/run/rollback/show` as a top-level command at
crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:442. Users following the README take
the longer `ft robot tx ...` path unaware that the shorter human form
exists.

ft-ijbql: README's workspace-structure block (lines 855-868) has
three stale count claims: vendored crates 47 (actual 42), fuzz
targets 4 (actual 37), doc files 114 (actual 258). The fuzz
understatement in particular sells the project's actual coverage
short.

Audit ran in <30 minutes against README.md + docs/proposals/ +
30-day git log. Beads are P2 task type, [reality-check] prefix.
Stopped at 2 findings — known/intentional gaps (frankensqlite vs
append_log, sub-50ms hedge, ft-y0loj monolith split, runtime_async
mediation, robot.not_implemented families) were already documented
in the README itself with explicit hedges and excluded from the
audit per instruction.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -217,7 +217,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-dlyhk","title":"[HIGH] wa.wait_for bypasses ReadOutput policy gate (crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:1618-1684)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:12:43.691734Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T18:23:10.273730Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T18:23:10.273458Z","close_reason":"Resolved via 868af5ad: ReadOutput policy gate applied to wa.wait_for before waiting.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","review","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-dqsev","title":"[bug] delete_session runs 3 DELETEs without a transaction — partial failure yields orphaned checkpoints","description":"# Bug

\`session_restore::delete_session\` at \`crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:1003\`:

\`\`\`rust
pub fn delete_session(db_path: &str, session_id: &str) -> Result<bool, RestoreError> {
    let conn = open_conn(db_path)?;
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id IN ...", [session_id])?;
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    let deleted = conn.execute("DELETE FROM mux_sessions WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    Ok(deleted > 0)
}
\`\`\`

Three independent DELETEs, each in its own implicit rusqlite
transaction. If step 1 succeeds but step 2 fails (lock contention,
disk full, IO error, cancellation), we leave the database in a
tornn-inconsistent state:

  - Option A: pane_states DELETED, checkpoints still present,
    session still present → \`load_checkpoint_by_id\` returns
    Some(checkpoint) but with empty \`pane_states\` → restore
    produces an empty-pane session that looks clean but carries no
    terminal state.

  - Option B: pane_states + checkpoints DELETED, session row
    still present → \`list_sessions\` shows the session, but
    \`load_latest_checkpoint\` returns None. Next \`delete_session\`
    call's third DELETE succeeds → session is eventually cleaned
    up, but the window is observable.

The other deletion-style paths in session_restore.rs DO wrap their
mutations in \`conn.transaction()\` (see lines 626, 673). delete_session
is the anomaly.

# Fix

Wrap the three DELETEs in a single \`conn.transaction()\` that
commits on success or rolls back on any intermediate error.

\`\`\`rust
pub fn delete_session(db_path: &str, session_id: &str) -> Result<bool, RestoreError> {
    let mut conn = open_conn(db_path)?;
    let tx = conn.transaction()?;
    tx.execute("DELETE FROM mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id IN ...", [session_id])?;
    tx.execute("DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    let deleted = tx.execute("DELETE FROM mux_sessions WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    tx.commit()?;
    Ok(deleted > 0)
}
\`\`\`

# Test

Regression test: inject mid-operation failure (e.g., by DELETING a
session that references a checkpoint whose pane_state rows have a
trigger or hook that fails). Without a transaction, the partial
state is observable; with the fix, the session row remains.

For a simpler test, assert that after a successful delete_session
there are zero rows in all three tables for the target session_id,
AND that commit/rollback atomicity holds across a wrapping PRAGMA.

# Scope

Single function, ~6 LOC change. No schema migration. Preserves the
return value contract (true if session row was deleted).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:06.907980Z","created_by":"pane3-delete-tx","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:53:29.944931Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:53:29.944608Z","close_reason":"Landed in 1a090629. Wrapped delete_session's three DELETE statements in a single conn.transaction() with commit on success. Pattern lifted from existing transactional helpers at session_restore.rs:626 + :673. Full cargo test blocked on ft-golgl pre-existing breakage — change is mechanical and preserves the return-value contract.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["atomicity","session","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-ds2ow","title":"[review][LOW] build.rs treats empty SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH as set, produces FT_BUILD_TS=epoch: and suppresses dirty check","description":"crates/frankenterm/build.rs:11 uses std::env::var("SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH").ok() — returns Ok("") when env var is exported empty (export SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=), not Err(NotPresent). Rest of file treats Some("") same as Some("1700000000"): line 25 suppresses git-dirty check, line 43-44 match Some("") → format_epoch_utc("") → both date invocations fail → falls to "epoch:" literal. Downstream ft version --full shows `built: epoch:` which passes label-presence tests at main.rs:53702 but is wrong semantically. Spec says SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH MUST be non-negative integer. Fix: .ok().filter(|s| !s.is_empty()). Audit round 6.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:32:37.192815Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:34:43.910751Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T18:34:43.910506Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 26841c83: build.rs now uses .ok().filter(|s| !s.trim().is_empty()) so SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH='' and whitespace-only values collapse to None (fallback to wall-clock). Some('0') preserved as a valid epoch. Code comment documents the rationale. Build.rs has no cargo-test-reachable helpers, so no unit test added; verified via standalone rustc --edition 2024 parse + runtime dry-run on ['', '   ', '0', '1700000000', None].","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
-{"id":"ft-dvgzi","title":"[no-mocks-test] crates/frankenterm-core/tests/snapshot_e2e.rs: 2058-line "e2e" suite uses MockWezterm — should drive a real WeztermClient subprocess for snapshot/restore","description":"## Mock at issue
crates/frankenterm-core/tests/snapshot_e2e.rs (2058 lines) is named e2e but
substitutes MockWezterm for the real pane mux at every test entry point:
  20: use frankenterm_core::wezterm::{MockWezterm, ...};
  752, 1073, 1302, 1599, 1773, 2017: let wezterm = Arc::new(MockWezterm::new());

## Why the mock is risky
Snapshot/restore is the durability contract for pane state: scrollback
bytes, layout tree, cursor, selection, IME composition, etc. With a mock
wezterm the assertions verify "we wrote some bytes" rather than "a freshly-
spawned wezterm pane restored to the snapshotted state matches the
original frame-for-frame." The 2026-04-26 e2e_replay-related failures and
the ft-1memj.28 split-divider geometry pin are exactly the category of
bug the mock cannot surface.

Mock Risk score: 5 (data: lose pane state) × 5 (MockWezterm dramatically
simpler than real WeztermClient) = 25 → MUST be mock-free per the no-mocks
skill matrix.

## Real fixture available
crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:859 defines `WeztermClient`
(pub struct, real implementation) and line 2606 `impl WeztermInterface
for WeztermClient`. A test-time fixture spawning `wezterm-mux-server`
(or a thin headless equivalent) via std::process::Command and
attaching WeztermClient is feasible. tests/common/lab.rs already
hosts the harness scaffolding.

## Suggested approach
1. Add `WeztermSubprocessFixture` to tests/common/lab.rs that spawns a
   real wezterm-mux-server + WeztermClient pair, exposes a
   `WeztermHandle`, and tears down the subprocess on Drop.
2. Migrate ONE snapshot_e2e test at a time to the real fixture.
3. Keep the MockWezterm path for the ~50% of tests that probe purely
   layout/protocol logic (those score < 8 and are fine to mock).
4. Land structured JSON-line logging (phase=spawn|snapshot|restore|verify,
   ms, byte counts, frame deltas) so CI failures are debuggable.
5. Pin the migration with a regression test: take a snapshot of pane
   with non-trivial scrollback (10k lines) + cursor mid-OSC + active
   selection, restart the mux subprocess, restore from snapshot, diff
   the restored frame against the original.

## Acceptance
- ≥3 snapshot_e2e tests run against a real wezterm-mux subprocess.
- Diff between original and restored frame ≤ comparator tolerance.
- No regression: existing MockWezterm tests still green.

## Out of scope
- Migrating the entire 2058-line file (incremental).
- Modifying WeztermClient or MockWezterm themselves.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-28T05:11:17.294418Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T05:11:17.294418Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["no-mocks","session-persistence","snapshot","test"]}

2. Migrate ONE snapshot_e2e test at a time to the real fixture.
3. Keep the MockWezterm path for the ~50% of tests that probe purely

4. Land structured JSON-line logging (phase=spawn|snapshot|restore|verify,

- Migrating the entire 2058-line file (incremental).
- Modifying WeztermClient or MockWezterm themselves.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc_2","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-28T05:11:17.294418Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T05:14:20.646158Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["no-mocks","session-persistence","snapshot","test"]}
 {"id":"ft-e018k","title":"event_identity_key nested Object insertion order leaks into hash (recursive fix for 765743d5)","description":"765743d5 sorted top-level keys of extracted object, but nested Object/Array values went through serde_json::to_string which preserves IndexMap insertion order. Two logically-equivalent events with nested {'context': {'a':1,'b':2}} vs {'context': {'b':2,'a':1}} hashed differently → dedup-to-two. Fix landed in 50ff1eb0: canonicalize_json_value recursively sorts all nested Object keys before serialization (arrays stay ordered per JSON spec, but their contained Objects get canonicalized). Regression tests event_identity_key_ignores_nested_extracted_object_insertion_order + event_identity_key_canonicalizes_objects_inside_arrays.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:08:33.936724Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T21:09:25.227773Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T21:09:25.227512Z","close_reason":"Landed in 50ff1eb0 (fix(events): recursively canonicalize nested Object keys in normalized_extracted)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-e0ssu","title":"policy rate limiter global rejection creates empty per-pane buckets","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:39:37.455407Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:19.194643Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:19.193768Z","close_reason":"Prevented global-limit rejections from allocating empty per-pane buckets, pruned empty stale pane buckets during check, and added a regression that repeated global rejections do not leak pane entries.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-e1emx","title":"[ft-kuxho/C] Spike: tail-padded PDU optional-fields encoding round-trip through varbincode","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-25T23:57:50.605357Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T02:30:01.935506Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T02:30:01.935265Z","close_reason":"added parameterized PDU conformance harness (custom_pdu_conformance_matrix) covering all 10 custom PDUs IDs 63-72 across canonical equality / encode determinism / tail-padded decode / 3 compression modes; targeted skip_serializing_if regression guard on UpdatePaneConstraints Option<usize> fields. 120/120 codec lib tests pass (commit pending)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","codec","spike"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-e1emx","depends_on_id":"ft-kuxho","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-25T23:58:10.408465Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
@@ -296,6 +296,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-icreu","title":"[MEDIUM] ActivityProfile::new allows NaN alpha/default_activity past clamp; poisons all hourly_ewma slots","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/survival.rs:437-438` — `ActivityProfile::new`.

ft-26k3h just hardened `update_hour` against NaN input. The construction-time path is the **complement**: `alpha.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` and `default_activity.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` both still let NaN through because `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _)` returns NaN.

Two independent failure modes:

1. **NaN `default_activity`**: all 24 `hourly_ewma` slots are initialised to NaN. `predict_hour` returns NaN for every slot until the first clean `update_hour` overwrites the bucket via the `sample_count == 0` branch. Until then, every hazard/survival score computed from `predict` silently produces NaN (matches the ft-26k3h failure shape but from a different path).

2. **NaN `alpha`**: `self.alpha = NaN`. `update_hour` at line 460 computes `alpha.mul_add(sample, (1.0 - alpha) * prev) = NaN` on every non-first sample. `hourly_ewma[idx]` stays NaN from the second sample onward. ft-26k3h's entry guard doesn't catch this because the caller passes a clean `normalized_activity` — the NaN is stored in `self.alpha`, not in the input.

Both are the same `f64::clamp(NaN)` gotcha shipped across ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5 / ft-x50ce / ft-26k3h. This closes the pair's construction-time counterpart.

## Fix

Snap NaN to sensible defaults at the boundary:
- `alpha` → 0.1 (a conservative EWMA coefficient; config-adjacent code uses 0.3 as the common default but 0.1 is safe for an unspecified sentinel).
- `default_activity` → 0.5 (neutral midpoint — if operator didn't supply a meaningful default, 0.5 is the least-surprising fallback).

Both `.is_nan()` checks happen **before** the clamp so the clamp still trims out-of-range finite inputs normally.

## Tests

* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_alpha` — alpha=NaN → stored α=0.1, EWMA updates stay finite.
* `ft_icreu_new_sanitises_nan_default_activity` — default=NaN → all hourly_ewma slots = 0.5, predict_hour returns 0.5.

## Scope

Single file (`survival.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:55:55.543754Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995785Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:58:20.995504Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ih4tm","title":"[MEDIUM] defer_fts_triggers=false is not reversible: triggers stay dropped after enabling once (ft-wk5fo follow-up)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs`.

d74401c6 landed `StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers` (ft-wk5fo). The intent is a reversible opt-in: flip to `true` to trade FTS freshness for write-path latency, flip back to `false` to restore synchronous indexing. The inline comment at line 6006 says as much:

> The triggers can be re-created by re-opening with `defer_fts_triggers: false`

That claim is wrong. Concrete interleaving:

1. Operator opens DB with `defer_fts_triggers: true` on an up-to-date schema (`user_version == SCHEMA_VERSION`).
2. `initialize_schema` returns early at line 4315 (no SQL executed, up-to-date path).
3. The explicit `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS output_segments_ai/ad/au` at line 6010 runs — triggers gone.
4. Operator flips config to `defer_fts_triggers: false` and restarts.
5. `initialize_schema` again short-circuits at line 4315.
6. Line 6010 is skipped (flag is false). **No `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS` runs.**
7. Database silently stays in deferred mode; the operator's `false` setting is a no-op.

The inline comment at line 6007 hints the author knew: 'this commit ships the opt-in direction only; reverting would require re-running SCHEMA_SQL or the operator dropping the DB.' That matches reality but contradicts the line-6006 'can be re-created by re-opening' claim — and leaves the config behaving non-intuitively for operators.

## Severity rationale

- Not a crash or data loss.
- Silent wrong answer: operator flips back to `false`, expects synchronous FTS, gets deferred.
- One-way-door config is a known anti-pattern. Easy fix.

## Fix sketch

Make the open path truly bidirectional. On every open, after `initialize_schema` returns:

* If `defer_fts_triggers == true`: `DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS ...` (idempotent).
* If `defer_fts_triggers == false`: `CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS ...` using the same SQL the SCHEMA_SQL block defines.

Factor the trigger SQL into one private const referenced by both branches so SCHEMA_SQL and the re-create branch cannot drift.

## Tests

* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_roundtrip — open with true, verify triggers dropped, close, reopen with false, verify triggers re-created.
* ft_ih4tm_defer_fts_triggers_idempotent_reopen — open with same flag twice back-to-back, no spurious SQL errors, trigger state stable.

## Scope

Single file (`storage.rs`). No API break (`defer_fts_triggers` already public, default `false`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:12.433144Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095316Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:31:50.095017Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ii8ss","title":"[security] wa.get_text 'tail' field has no upper bound — user-controlled usize allows unbounded scrollback fetch (memory-pressure vector); apply the LIMIT_MIN/LIMIT_MAX pattern from wa.events","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-26T06:39:39.458126Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T07:21:39.223347Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T07:21:39.222765Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-ijbql","title":"[reality-check] README workspace-structure counts are stale (vendored 47→42, fuzz 4→37, docs 114→258)","description":"## Gap

README.md's 'Workspace Structure' section (around lines 855-868) makes three
specific count claims that are off by enough to mislead readers about scale.

| Claim | README line | README says | Reality | Drift |
|-------|-------------|-------------|---------|-------|
| Vendored crates | 857 | `frankenterm/  # In-tree vendored workspace crates (47 crates)` | 42 | -5 |
| Fuzz targets | 865 | `fuzz/  # 4 fuzzing targets` | 37 | +33 |
| Doc files | 866 | `docs/  # 114 documentation files` | 258 | +144 |

## Reality (HEAD verification)

```bash
$ find frankenterm -maxdepth 2 -name Cargo.toml | wc -l
42
$ find fuzz/fuzz_targets -name "*.rs" | wc -l
37
$ find docs -name "*.md" | wc -l
258
```

The fuzz and docs counts are dramatically understated; the vendored count is
slightly overstated.

## Why this matters

- The README's "How ft Compares" table (line 258) and "Why Use ft?" pitch
  rely on scale credibility. Telling readers there are 4 fuzz targets when
  there are 37 sells the project short.
- "114 documentation files" suggests the docs tree is small/manageable;
  258 is a different conversation about what's worth indexing.
- Users following `find frankenterm -maxdepth 2 -name Cargo.toml | wc -l`
  (the README itself recommends this exact command at line 38 of the
  'Platform Model' subsection) will see 42, not 47.

## Suggested fix

Either:
1. **Replace static counts with the live commands** the README already
   recommends elsewhere (`find ... | wc -l`). Self-updating, no future drift.
2. **Update the static counts** to match HEAD (42 / 37 / 258) and add a
   build-time check (a small CI step that re-runs the counts and fails if
   they drift by more than ±10%).

Option 1 is preferred: zero maintenance burden, and the user gets the truth
at the moment they read it.

## Out of scope

- Reorganizing the workspace itself.
- Reducing the number of fuzz targets or doc files.
- The 'tests/e2e # 149 end-to-end test harnesses' line at line 867 — that
  count needs separate verification (tests/e2e currently has 198 .sh scripts
  and 1 .py harness, but 'harness' may have a narrower meaning here).

## Verification

After fix, the three commands above must agree with the README text (or
the README must use the commands themselves).

Filed by cc_1 reality-check audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-28T05:14:12.979874Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T05:14:12.979874Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-iqwt5","title":"perf(storage): avoid per-pane no-op FTS catchup on healthy startup","description":"Hot path: crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:16959-16983 lists every distinct pane_id and calls sync_fts_for_pane for each pane on every healthy startup. sync_fts_for_pane then does progress lookup plus an unindexed-segment query even when the pane has no new output, making startup O(number_of_panes) SQL round trips for a fully caught-up FTS index. Full rebuild has the same pane list pattern at crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:16813-16837, where it is expected, but incremental healthy startup can be narrowed.

Concrete optimization: replace the incremental pane list with a single query that joins output_segments against fts_pane_progress and returns only panes where MAX(seq) is greater than last_indexed_seq or progress is absent. Keep the seq=0 first-pane sentinel behavior by including panes with no progress. Add a regression test with many caught-up panes and one dirty pane asserting sync_fts_for_pane is invoked only for the dirty/missing-progress set.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"greyfalcon","created_at":"2026-04-27T19:33:17.375376Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-27T20:09:34.076966Z","closed_at":"2026-04-27T20:09:34.076705Z","close_reason":"Selective FTS startup sync landed in 6a0590d2 and targeted regression passes","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ismux","title":"[mission][dispatch] MissionDispatcher reports accepted dispatch without calling WorkflowRunner::handle_detection","description":"In `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:200-226`, `dispatch_single()` builds a synthetic detection, then only calls `runner.find_matching_workflow(&detection)` and fabricates `accepted: true` plus a synthetic `execution_id` when a workflow exists. It never invokes `WorkflowRunner::handle_detection()`, so no lock acquisition, no concurrency-limit enforcement, no storage row, and no real workflow start happen.

Impact: callers receive a success-shaped `DispatchResult` that claims work was accepted even though the runner was never asked to start anything. This also makes the existing close reason on `ft-y9lnb.3` overstated relative to the current code.

Fix direction: bridge the sync mission loop to the async runner start path with a bounded runtime, map the actual `WorkflowStartResult` into `DispatchResult`, and only emit started metadata from a real start result.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:05:13.673841Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:13:49.575203Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:13:49.574685Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-4r2ib. crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs is already dirty in this checkout with the exact handle_detection/WorkflowStartResult fix and regression coverage, so I did not collide with pane-4-owned WIP.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-iwlsh","title":"[perf][scan-pipeline] scan_locate allocates a per-call Vec on the chunked hot path; the caller never needs it","description":"Audit-pass profiling finding (AmberFox 2026-04-26). scan_pipeline.rs:394 is the ONLY production caller of TriggerScanner::scan_locate (the other 4 callers are inside #[cfg(test)] blocks). The call shape is:

  for matched in self.trigger_scanner.scan_locate(scratch) {
      if matched.offset.saturating_add(matched.length) <= overlap_len {
          continue;
      }
      ...
  }

scan_locate (pattern_trigger.rs:589-595) is a thin wrapper that allocates a fresh Vec::new() and then calls for_each_leftmost_match to push matches into it; the caller iterates and consumes the vec. The Vec is never retained. For high-match-rate inputs (compiler output with thousands of warnings, log streams full of ERROR markers) this is O(matches) allocation traffic on the same hot path ft-6db1t addressed for the trigger-counts HashMap.

Fix: rewrite scan_pipeline.rs:394 to use for_each_leftmost_match directly with a closure that performs the offset filter + array-indexed increment. Eliminates the per-call Vec allocation entirely. The wrapper scan_locate stays for the 4 test callers + external API.

Same proven pattern as ft-6db1t (TriggerCategoryCounts replacing HashMap<TriggerCategory, u64>): closure-driven hot path, allocation kept in the test-only convenience method.

Estimated impact: per-chunk allocation eliminated. On high-frequency capture paths (200+-pane fleets, log-heavy panes) this is amortized but non-trivial.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T22:44:01.861265Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T23:07:47.133725Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T23:07:47.133244Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-x50ce","title":"[MEDIUM] conformal::ConformalPredictor silently narrows prediction intervals when coverage=NaN","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/conformal.rs:286` — `ConformalPredictor::new` stores `coverage: coverage.clamp(0.01, 0.999)`.

Same `f64::clamp(NaN, _, _) = NaN` gotcha as ft-761tz / ft-b4l62 / ft-jz40p / ft-0p5q5. When `coverage = NaN`, the stored value stays NaN. On every `forecast_all` call:

1. `cal.quantile(self.coverage)` (line 326) is called with NaN coverage.
2. `CalibrationBuffer::quantile` (line 213) computes `k = ((coverage * (n+1)).ceil() as usize).max(1)` → `NaN.ceil() = NaN` → `NaN as usize = 0` → `k = 1`.
3. `k > n` is false (n ≥ 1), so falls through.
4. Returns `sorted[0]` — the **smallest** calibration score.
5. `forecast_all` wraps `(point - q, point + q)` using the smallest q possible — producing the **narrowest** prediction interval the calibration data can support.

**Net**: operators set `coverage = NaN` (via config typo, arithmetic bug upstream, deserialization of an invalid input), and the predictor silently undercuts the formal-coverage guarantee by emitting the tightest possible intervals instead of the widest. Intervals labelled `coverage: NaN` in the output look broken but are still shipped; downstream alerting thresholds driven by these intervals over-fire.

## Severity rationale

- Silent statistical wrong-answer: the predictor's entire point is the formal coverage guarantee; NaN undermines it silently.
- Not a crash or data loss.
- Input reachable via config deserialization or external arithmetic (e.g., `1.0 - alpha` where alpha came from a NaN upstream).

## Fix

Guard at the new() boundary: if `coverage.is_nan()`, snap to the default 0.95 (same as `ConformalConfig::default()` at line 76). Alternative would be to reject and return Result, but callers signature is fn new() -> Self, so keep the infallible default. A tracing::warn log helps debug.

Also tighten `CalibrationBuffer::quantile` at line 213 so even if `self.coverage` is ever NaN (future code paths, direct callers), the function returns None instead of silently-smallest. Same pattern as ft-b4l62/ft-jz40p.

## Tests

* `ft_x50ce_predictor_rejects_nan_coverage_at_construction` — `new(..., coverage: NaN, ..)` stores 0.95 default, not NaN.
* `ft_x50ce_quantile_nan_coverage_returns_none` — `CalibrationBuffer::quantile(NaN)` returns None (defense in depth).

## Scope

Single file (`conformal.rs`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:29:12.968247Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:33:02.964336Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:33:02.963917Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-x5tx2","title":"[EPIC] Eliminate verified first-party placeholders and stubs","description":"# Goal
Eliminate every verified first-party placeholder, stub, mock-like runtime branch, and misleading partial implementation surfaced by the 2026-04-02 audit.

Every in-scope item must end in exactly one of two states:
1. Real shipping behavior with tests, metrics, and operator-visible semantics.
2. Explicitly narrowed scope backed by code changes, docs, and guardrails so the code no longer masquerades as implemented behavior.

# Inventory Covered By This Epic
- CLI / operator support: `build_ntm_stub_response`, `chrono_stub_now`, scheduled backup compression fallback.
- Support crates: `frankenterm/env-bootstrap` and `ringlog` bootstrap/logger stubs.
- Client/runtime control plane: `ActiveWorkspaceChanged` TODO, unilateral-message FIXME, `spawn_pane` unimplemented, alt-screen hardcoded false.
- SDK / connectors / search: transparency verification stub, certification integration probe stub, generated Rust SDK transport unimplemented, `CrossEncoderReranker` no-op, `Model2VecEmbedder` stub, FastEmbed sentinel constructor behavior.
- Core runtime / UX: `NullPaneScrollbackAccess`, FTUI placeholder views, recording delta encodings, stale PaneCapabilities stub annotation.
- Rendering / windowing: Wayland seat+keyboard TODOs, WGL/macOS/EGL resize gaps, bitmap/WebGPU readback gaps, terminfo raw/cropped image gaps, GUI background/glyph placeholders.
- Mux / SSH: tmux PTY lifecycle gaps, detach unimplemented across domains, SFTP metadata mutation, SSH `Match Exec` evaluation.

# Scope Boundary
Only first-party source is in scope. Test doubles under `#[cfg(test)]`, intentional null objects, unsupported-platform no-op shims, and vendored upstream TODOs are handled only if this program reclassifies them with evidence.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:25:58.707216Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T02:31:29.588832Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T02:31:29.587715Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["placeholder-remediation","stub-audit"]}
 {"id":"ft-x86z2","title":"[security][P1] 8 mutating MCP tools skip PolicyEngine.authorize (tx_run/rollback, mission_abort/pause/resume, events_annotate/triage/label)","description":"## Finding

Audit of crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs found 8 mutating MCP tools that do NOT invoke PolicyEngine.authorize, while their peers do.

### Tools with policy gates (grep-verified)
- wa.send (:2009), wa.workflow_run (:2242), wa.reserve (:3127), wa.release (:3259), wa.accounts_refresh (:3499)

### Tools MISSING policy gates
- wa.tx_run (:2607-:2825) — prepare+commit, persists receipts
- wa.tx_rollback (:2825-:2986) — compensation phase
- wa.mission_abort (:4132+) — aborts mission
- wa.mission_pause (:3870+) — persists state transition
- wa.mission_resume (:3994+) — persists state transition
- wa.events_annotate (:4260+) — writes event_notes (also TOCTOU-racy per ft-xo3u4)
- wa.events_triage (:4409+) — writes event_triage_state
- wa.events_label (:4567+) — writes event_labels

## Why this matters

PolicyEngine.authorize enforces rate limits, approval requirements, capability gates, and audit-trail-before-side-effect across ALL mutations. The existing gated tools call it; these 8 skip it. Compromised or misbehaving MCP clients can abort missions, rollback transactions, and overwrite event annotations without any allow/deny/require-approval evaluation. Audit records (some tools call record_audit_action_redacted) are AFTER-the-fact and don't substitute for authorization BEFORE the mutation.

## Fix

Each handler: add engine.authorize(&input) before the mutation, matching the pattern at :2009/:2242/:3127/:3259/:3499. Deny → return MCP_ERR_POLICY_DENIED envelope. RequireApproval → return MCP_ERR_REQUIRE_APPROVAL envelope.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 8 tools gated.
- New test per tool: deny-config policy causes deny-envelope instead of mutation.
- Default allow-policy tests continue to pass.

## Testing
Per-tool integration test calling the tool under a deny-config, asserting envelope.ok=false + appropriate error_code.

## Severity rationale
P1 (not P0): MCP ships over stdio/localhost by default, so remote exploitation requires operator opt-in to networked MCP. But wa.events_annotate/_triage/_label are used in multi-agent coordination flows — a malicious peer agent abusing them to clobber handoff notes is a realistic trust-boundary break. Fix is mechanical.

Surfaced by RusticMeadow during /security-audit-for-saas sweep, 2026-04-23.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:04:41.658155Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:12:05.326207Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:12:05.325259Z","close_reason":"Partially fixed in a4b7c8c3: 5 of the 8 ungated mutating MCP tools now call PolicyEngine.authorize BEFORE any side effect — wa.tx_run, wa.tx_rollback, wa.mission_pause, wa.mission_resume, wa.mission_abort. New helper mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation at mcp_tools.rs:~177 builds a PolicyInput(ExecCommand, Mcp) with PolicySurface::Mcp, runs the engine, returns MCP_ERR_POLICY envelope on Deny or RequireApproval. Gate fires before contract load / mission load / state persist in each handler.

Remaining 3 tools (wa.events_annotate/_triage/_label) have struct fields db_path:Arc<PathBuf> with no Arc<Config>, so adding the gate is a mechanical constructor refactor touching mcp_bridge.rs + ~10 test call sites — filed as ft-vue5t (P2) so this audit bead doesn't block on the refactor.

Known limitation (documented in commit): gate returns MCP_ERR_POLICY with a hint on RequireApproval instead of issuing an allow-once token via ApprovalStore.attach_to_decision (which wa.workflow_run at :2242 does). The handlers don't have an async storage handle in scope at the gate point, and wiring one was out of scope for this 60-min slot. Upgrading the gate to attach_to_decision is a separate follow-up.

Default-policy tests continue to pass (allow-by-default never enters the early-return branch). Per-tool deny-policy tests scoped into a future pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","mcp","policy","security"]}
+{"id":"ft-xaych","title":"[reality-check] README Tx section recommends ft robot tx but ft tx exists as top-level command","description":"## Gap

README.md at lines 496-507 ('Mission & Tx Control' section) tells users:

> Tx lifecycle commands currently ship under robot mode

…and shows examples that go through the verbose `ft robot tx ...` form
exclusively:

```
ft robot tx plan
ft robot tx run
ft robot tx run --fail-step tx-step:commit
ft robot tx rollback
ft robot tx show --include-contract
```

## Reality

`ft tx` is a top-level command per `crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:442`:

```rust
Tx {
    #[command(subcommand)]
    command: TxCommands,
},
```

The `clap` `after_help` block on the top-level Tx variant (main.rs:432) even
documents the user-facing CLI:

```
EXAMPLES:
    ft tx plan                         Validate tx contract and summarize lifecycle
    ft tx run                          Execute deterministic prepare+commit flow
    ft tx run --fail-step tx-step:2    Inject a deterministic commit failure
    ft tx rollback                     Execute compensation phase for committed steps
    ft tx show --include-contract      Inspect receipts and full contract payload
```

Both forms work — `ft tx ...` (top-level, human-friendly) and `ft robot tx ...`
(JSON output for agents). The README is steering users toward the verbose
robot form unaware that the shorter form exists.

## Why this matters

- The README's Quick Example (line 158) also uses `ft robot tx run` even when
  the surrounding example is otherwise human-driven.
- Users who don't need JSON output get a longer command than necessary.
- The 'Mission & Tx Control' header section header reads as if Tx wasn't
  promoted to top-level yet — but it has been.

## Suggested fix

Update README.md's `### Mission & Tx Control` block to lead with `ft tx ...`
(the top-level form) as the primary example, with `ft robot tx ...` mentioned
as the JSON-output sibling for agents/scripts. Remove the
'currently ship under robot mode' hedge.

Quick Example block (line 158) should also use `ft tx run` for the
human-driven example.

## Verification

- `grep -n "Tx {" crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs` shows the top-level Tx variant at line 442.
- `grep -n 'ft tx' README.md` shows zero hits today; it should show several after the fix.

## Out of scope

- Renaming or removing `ft robot tx ...` (it's the right surface for agents).
- Changing CLI behavior — only README copy-edits.

Filed by cc_1 reality-check audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-28T05:13:54.619158Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T05:13:54.619158Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-xbnl0","title":"[EPIC] Goal-line closure for FrankenTerm: native Asupersync, zero fake capabilities, and multi-day swarm reliability","description":"# Goal
Close the original FrankenTerm mission all the way to a credible finish line: a swarm-native terminal and control plane that can replace fragile legacy multi-agent terminal workflows, survive large multi-day agent workloads, and stop depending on dishonest capability claims or legacy async runtime debt.

This epic exists because the repo is already substantial, but it still has three kinds of unfinished work that make basically done false.
1. Native async migration is incomplete; direct `tokio`, `smol`, `async-io`, and `runtime_compat` quarantine still shape real behavior.
2. Several supported-path surfaces still advertise capabilities that are stubbed, placeholder-quality, or explicitly `todo!()` / `unimplemented!()`.
3. The mux runtime stack has not yet been proved against the original mission: multi-day, many-agent, leak-resistant, operator-trustworthy operation with evidence rather than hope.

# Program Boundary
This program does not replace existing migration or placeholder beads. It is the finish-line wrapper that turns those scattered efforts into one closure graph with explicit sequencing, evidence requirements, and a final release bar.","design":"# Sequencing
1. Establish truth first: inventories, ownership, dependency hygiene, and a concrete release bar.
2. Finish the native Asupersync cutover the right way: `&Cx`-first APIs, region-owned tasks, shrinking compat boundaries, deterministic tests.
3. Close supported-path capability lies: either implement the behavior for real or narrow the surface explicitly so the code stops pretending.
4. Prove reliability with instrumentation, leak-oracle regressions, soak evidence, and performance budgets on workloads that resemble actual swarm use.
5. Freeze the result with docs, operator guidance, CI, doctor, audit gates, and a final evidence bundle.

# Architectural Intent
The project is done when the core control plane is native, the advertised capability surface is honest, and the original multi-agent over-days operating model is demonstrated with stored evidence. Compile success alone is not a finish line.","acceptance_criteria":"1. All five child epics are closed with explicit evidence recorded in their completion trails.
2. Supported builds and shipping paths no longer depend on direct `tokio`, `smol`, or `async-io` usage, except for any deliberately documented quarantine that is itself covered by an open bead in this program.
3. No supported-path placeholder or fake capability remains without an explicit narrowed contract and regression guard.
4. Mux and runtime leak-proofing, soak confidence, and performance budgets have hard evidence attached, not just anecdotal local runs.
5. README, operator docs, and guardrails reflect the final system rather than migration-era aspirations.
6. Every non-planning finish-line implementation or proof bead closes against the shared verification contract in `ft-xbnl0.1.4`, with exact `rch exec -- ...` proof commands and stored artifacts.","notes":"Use this epic as the authoritative wrapper for the final push. Older beads stay where they are, but their closure now has to satisfy this program's release bar.

All non-planning children of this program should treat `ft-xbnl0.1.4` as the shared verification contract. Heavy cargo verification and proof runs that rely on remote execution should also cite `ft-xbnl0.1.5` so future maintainers know the remote substrate was part of the closure story.

Canonical contract document: `docs/ft-xbnl0-verification-contract.md`.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:07:15.833371Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T05:48:02.595502Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T05:48:02.595334Z","close_reason":"All 5 child epics and blockers closed - goal-line program rollup complete","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","goal-line","mux","release-readiness","reliability","swarm"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0","depends_on_id":"ft-d0ez0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:58:59.919450Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0","depends_on_id":"ft-y9lnb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:58:59.698012Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2816,"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0","author":"IndigoBear","text":"Sequencing rule for the whole finish-line program:
1. Establish truth inventories and normalize the backlog.
2. Finish native Asupersync cutover.
3. Close supported-path fake/unimplemented behavior.
4. Prove the original multi-day swarm mission with leak instrumentation, deterministic regressions, and soak evidence.
5. Freeze the result with docs, guards, operator acceptance, and a final closure bundle.

The project is not “done” at compile success. It is done only when all five layers are complete.","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:09:53Z"},{"id":2817,"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0","author":"OliveDesert","text":"Created from the goal-line planning request to replace dependence on the old markdown plan with a bead-native finish-line program.

1. The new graph intentionally wraps inherited blockers instead of replacing them, so existing implementation work remains traceable.
2. Two important gaps that were not cleanly represented before now have explicit ownership: the remaining mux-server `sessionhandler` operations and the long-haul reliability proof lane.
3. The root finish-line claim is stricter than compile success: native Asupersync closure, honest support claims, leak remediation, soak proof, release guards, and operator acceptance all have to land.","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:26:02Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.1","title":"[EPIC] Goal-line control: truth inventory, backlog normalization, and closure ledger","description":"# Goal
Establish a trustworthy execution map for the finish-line push so work is not guided by stale beads, grep folklore, or half-remembered architecture notes.

This epic exists because the remaining work is spread across old async-migration beads, the placeholder-remediation program, and uncovered gaps like mux-server sessionhandler behavior and leak-proofing. Before implementation, future agents need one self-contained ledger that answers four questions.
1. What is still unfinished?
2. Which surfaces are genuinely supported?
3. Which older beads are still authoritative?
4. What evidence is required before any lane can be called done?","design":"# Design
1. Build two truth inventories: one for async and runtime debt, one for supported-path placeholder and unimplemented debt.
2. Normalize the older graph rather than pretending it does not exist; link or de-duplicate beads instead of creating shadow plans.
3. Add an explicit evidence matrix so later closure is testable and auditable.
4. Keep this epic operational: it should be updated as new finish-line facts are discovered.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Every finish-line blocker is mapped either to an inherited bead or a concrete new bead in this program.
2. Every inherited dependency used by this program has current status, scope, and rationale that still make sense.
3. The finish-line evidence bar is written down in bead-native form, not only in chat history or markdown plans.
4. New work picked from this program can be understood without reopening the old markdown plan.","notes":"This is the control epic, not a paperwork tax. Its output should actively reduce wasted implementation effort and prevent done-in-one-place, still-open-somewhere-else confusion.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:09:51.657113Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-06T01:59:27.278872Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T01:59:27.278640Z","close_reason":"All 5 child beads closed (1.1-1.5): async/runtime inventory complete, supported-path audit complete, closure ledger normalized, verification contract defined, RCH worker parity stabilized.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["goal-line","inventory","ledger","planning"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-xbnl0.1","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:09:51.657113Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-xbnl0.1.1","title":"Inventory every direct tokio, smol, async-io, and compat surface and assign a native Asupersync owner","description":"# Goal
Produce the authoritative async-debt ledger for the finish-line push.

This task exists because the project still has multiple overlapping sources of truth for runtime debt: `Cargo.toml` manifests, direct imports, `runtime_compat`, hidden helper modules, and comments that describe old migration assumptions. The finish-line graph needs one bead-native inventory that names each active offender, where it lives, why it still exists, and which downstream bead owns removing it.","design":"# Design
1. Search manifests and source for direct `tokio`, `smol`, `async-io`, and compat-layer usage.
2. Classify each occurrence as one of three things: native replacement required, temporary quarantine boundary, or dead code or comment debt that should simply disappear.
3. Record the intended Asupersync replacement shape for each class, including whether the migration should use `&Cx`, `Scope`, native networking, native channels, or explicit adapter quarantine.
4. Link every inventoried surface to an inherited or newly-created owner bead.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Every direct async-runtime offender relevant to supported builds is mapped to a file or crate location, a migration class, and an owner bead.
2. The inventory distinguishes real shipping-path debt from test-only or intentionally quarantined holdouts.
3. The inventory is attached to this bead in durable bead-native form and cross-linked from downstream cutover tasks.
4. No downstream async bead in this program relies on undocumented mystery debt.","notes":"SilentSpire 2026-04-03: authoritative async-debt ledger for the finish-line async cutover.

Shipping-path classes
A. Direct vendored/client Smol + async-io surfaces -> native replacement required -> owner `ft-xbnl0.2.1` (inherits `ft-riz42`).
   files: `crates/frankenterm-mux-server-impl/src/dispatch.rs`; `frankenterm/client/src/client.rs`; `frankenterm/client/src/domain.rs`; `frankenterm/promise/src/spawn.rs`; `frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs`; `frankenterm/ssh/src/lib.rs`; `frankenterm/uds/src/lib.rs`; `frankenterm/async_ossl/src/lib.rs`.
   active usage shape confirmed by source search: `smol::Async`, `smol::channel`, `smol::future::or`, `smol::block_on`, `smol::Timer`, `smol::io::*`, plus `async_io::IoSafe` wrappers and `async_io::Timer` / `block_on` executor seams.
   replacement shape: native Asupersync socket or FD registration, `&Cx`-threaded APIs, scope-owned tasks, native channels, native timers, and no executor-neutral Smol shims.

B. Core Tokio quarantine in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime_compat.rs` -> temporary quarantine boundary to collapse -> primary owner `ft-xbnl0.2.2`, with transport/network sub-owners `ft-xbnl0.2.3` and `ft-xbnl0.2.4`, and final seam removal `ft-xbnl0.2.5`.
   root compat surface still covers locks, channels, spawn/join, time, Unix/TCP sockets, signal handling, and runtime builder utilities.
   current import blast radius: 77 files under `crates/frankenterm-core/src` + `crates/frankenterm/src` import `runtime_compat`; that count includes hot-path code plus audit/guard helpers, so ownership is partitioned below.
   `ft-xbnl0.2.2` hot-path files: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs`, `events.rs`, `search_bridge.rs`, `watchdog.rs`, `telemetry.rs`, `restore_process.rs`, `session_retention.rs`, `survival.rs`, `retry.rs`, `wait.rs`, `workflows/mod.rs`, `workflows/runner.rs`, `workflows/engine.rs`, `caut.rs`, `tui/query.rs`.
   `ft-xbnl0.2.3` local transport files: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/ipc.rs`, `native_events.rs`, `event_stream.rs` and related local control-stream helpers.
   `ft-xbnl0.2.4` network boundary files: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs`, `web/server.rs`, `web/sse.rs`, `web_framework.rs`.
   replacement shape: thread `&Cx` through owned async APIs, replace detached spawn with `Scope`, replace Tokio-shaped channels/locks/select/sleep with native Asupersync primitives, and leave only explicit named quarantine where a foreign boundary is still unavoidable.

C. Manifest and feature-seam debt -> owner `ft-xbnl0.2.5` for cleanup/collapse and `ft-xbnl0.2.6` for permanent enforcement.
